📖 Glossary

Overview

PsychologyThe scientific study of behaviour and mental processes.
StructuralismTitchener's school of thought focused on studying the structure of the mind.
FunctionalismWilliam James' school of thought focused on the functions of the mind.
BehaviorismWatson's school of thought focused on observable, measurable behaviour rather than mental processes.
DualismThe view that the mind is separate from the brain but somehow controls it.
MonismThe view that conscious experience is inseparable from the physical brain.
HypothesisA tentative, testable explanation of an event or relationship — an educated guess about behaviour.
Independent variableThe factor manipulated by the experimenter to observe its effect on behaviour.
Dependent variableThe outcome factor that may change in response to manipulation of the independent variable.
Placebo effectImprovement due to the expectation of improving, rather than an actual active treatment.
Naturalistic observationA research method where behaviour is observed in its natural setting without researcher intervention.
Correlation coefficientA statistic (-1.00 to +1.00) indicating the type and strength of relationship between two variables.

Theoretical Paradigms

Psychic determinismThe belief that all behaviour originates from unconscious childhood experiences.
IdFreud's structure containing innate sexual/aggressive instincts; operates on the pleasure principle.
EgoFreud's structure that mediates between the id and superego; operates on the reality principle.
SuperegoFreud's structure containing the morals and rules of society; operates on the morality principle.
Defense mechanismsStrategies (e.g. repression, projection, denial, regression) that protect the ego from anxiety.
Collective unconsciousJung's concept of inherited material from past generations that shapes personality via archetypes.
Classical conditioningPavlov's learning process where a neutral stimulus comes to elicit a response after pairing with an unconditioned stimulus.
Operant conditioningLearning through reinforcement and punishment; based on Thorndike's law of effect.
Observational learningBandura's concept of learning through observing and imitating others (also called modeling).
Self-actualizationMaslow's concept of the continuous desire to fulfil one's potential — the top of the needs hierarchy.
Unconditional positive regardOne of Rogers' core therapeutic conditions — accepting a client without judgment.
SchemaA cognitive structure containing organized knowledge that guides perception and interpretation.
Biopsychosocial(s) modelA model explaining behaviour via biological, psychological, and social factors (with a spiritual component).

Biological Foundations

Central Nervous System (CNS)The brain and spinal cord.
Peripheral Nervous System (PNS)Nerve branches from the spinal cord that reach the rest of the body.
Sympathetic nervous systemPrepares the body for emergencies/strenuous activity — speeds heart rate, suspends digestion.
Parasympathetic nervous systemConserves energy and returns the body to its resting state after activity.
NeuronThe elementary cell of the nervous system; transmits electrical/chemical signals.
Myelin sheathA white fatty substance insulating the axon, speeding transmission of nerve impulses.
SynapseThe gap between one neuron's axon terminals and another's dendrites, across which neurotransmitters are released.
DopamineA neurotransmitter involved in learning, attention, emotional arousal, and movement; linked to schizophrenia (excess) and Parkinson's (deficit).
SerotoninA neurotransmitter affecting mood, hunger, sleep, and arousal; low levels linked to depression.
HypothalamusA brain structure controlling hunger, thirst, sexual behaviour, and temperature regulation.
AmygdalaA limbic system structure controlling violent emotions such as rage and fear.
HippocampusA limbic system structure important in the formation of memories.
Cerebral cortexThe outer layer of the forebrain; site of conscious thinking, planning, reasoning, and language.
LateralizationThe specialization of function in one brain hemisphere, revealed by split-brain research.
Pituitary glandThe 'master gland' of the endocrine system; its secretions influence hormone release by other glands.
HeredityThe genetic transmission of characteristics from parents to offspring.

Motivation

MotivationThe force/energy that initiates, guides, and maintains goal-oriented behaviour.
Intrinsic motivationMotivation to perform an activity for its own inherent pleasure or value, not an external reward.
Extrinsic motivationMotivation to perform an activity to achieve an external goal or reward.
Drive theoryMotivation theory based on homeostasis — organisms act to reduce internal tension (drives).
Incentive theoryMotivation theory holding that external rewards/goals pull behaviour.
Arousal theoryMotivation theory holding that people act to maintain an optimal level of physiological arousal.
Yerkes-Dodson LawPerformance increases with arousal up to an optimal point, then decreases with further arousal.
LeptinA hormone made by fat cells that decreases appetite and signals sufficient energy stores.
GhrelinA hormone released mainly by the stomach that increases appetite and signals hunger.
